entry.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 in University Data Theft Campaign
entry.content:
This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability discovery may be compressing exploit timelines faster than traditional patch cycles can absorb. Google Mandiant attributed a PeopleSoft exploitation campaign to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, with observed activity dated between May 27 and June 9. The exploited vulnerability, CVE-2026-35273, affects Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools and carries a CVSS score of 9.8 due to unauthenticated remote code execution over HTTP without user interaction. The campaign appears heavily concentrated in higher education. Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, and 68% were in higher education, mostly in the United States. The University of Nottingham confirmed a breach, and Have I Been Pwned counted approximately 455,000 unique email addresses in a leaked dataset associated with the incident. Oracle confirmed that CVE-2026-35273 affects PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools versions 8.61 and 8.62 and released emergency mitigations while indicating that a patch is forthcoming. BleepingComputer reported that ShinyHunters claimed to have used a “gadget chain” combining older flaws and zero-day vulnerabilities, allegedly stealing data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations. Researcher Michael R also identified exposed online directories containing attack-related tooling and shared IP addresses relevant to the campaign. Cisco Talos highlighted analysis from Yuri Kramarz arguing that AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing human-centered patching processes. The article frames frontier AI models as capable of autonomously discovering and exploiting zero-days in minutes, compressing the traditional vulnerability lifecycle and increasing the operational pressure on defenders. The Talos discussion places the PeopleSoft activity in a broader context: organizations face a threat environment where exploit development, weaponization, and deployment may occur faster than conventional vulnerability management workflows can respond. The piece emphasizes foundational security controls, segmentation, behavioral detection, and validation exercises as part of a fallback model for environments where patch-first strategies may not keep pace with emerging exploitation timelines. Permiso announced that it won Best Identity Threat Detection and Response Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards, presented by The Hacker News. The item reflects continued industry focus on identity threat detection and response as a dedicated security category, particularly as credential misuse, cloud identity abuse, and post-compromise identity activity remain central to modern intrusion chains.

ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day (CVE-2026-35273) to Breach Universities — Must Read

ShinyHunters exploited a zero-day remote code execution flaw in Oracle PeopleSoft to breach universities and steal sensitive data before Oracle disclosed the issue. Organizations running PeopleSoft should immediately restrict or disable exposed PSEMHUB and related endpoints, apply available patches, and hunt for signs of compromise.

  • Google Mandiant attributes the Oracle PeopleSoft exploitation campaign to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, and dates the activity between May 27 and June 9.
  • CVE-2026-35273 is a 9.8-rated remote code execution flaw in Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools that requires no login or user interaction and can be exploited over HTTP.
  • Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, and 68% of them were in higher education, mostly in the United States.
  • The University of Nottingham confirmed a breach, and Have I Been Pwned counted about 455,000 unique email addresses in the leaked data set.
  • Oracle advised disabling or removing the Environment Management Hub service, or blocking external access to /PSEMHUB/* and /PSIGW/HttpListeningConnector if disabling is not possible.

A tale of two eras — Optional

Talos argues that AI-driven zero-day discovery is accelerating too fast for traditional patch-first security to keep up. Organizations should focus on resilience through MFA, hardening, segmentation, behavioral detection, and tested incident response.

  • Talos highlighted Yuri Kramarz’s blog arguing that AI-driven vulnerability discovery has outpaced human patching capabilities.
  • The article says frontier AI models can autonomously discover and exploit zero-days in minutes, collapsing the traditional vulnerability lifecycle.
  • Talos recommends organizations move away from patch-reliant strategies and adopt a three-stage fallback model based on foundational security principles.
  • Recommended controls include enforcing MFA, hardening devices with CIS benchmarks, and implementing strict network segmentation to limit an attacker’s blast radius.
  • The article advises deploying behavioral-based EDR, NDR, and XDR and validating controls through penetration testing and purple team exercises.

Permiso Wins Best Identity Threat Detection and Response (ITDR) Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards — Optional

Permiso announced that its Identity Threat Detection and Response platform won Best ITDR Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards presented by The Hacker News.

  • Permiso announced that it won Best Identity Threat Detection and Response Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards.
  • The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards are presented by The Hacker News.
  • The award recognized Permiso’s Identity Threat Detection and Response platform.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability...

entry.title: CalPhishing, The Gentlemen Ransomware, Miasma/Hades, and AI Agent OAuth Risk
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust boundaries around OAuth, prompt injection, and managed security operations. Health-ISAC reported an emerging healthcare-sector social engineering pattern called CalPhishing, in which attackers use iCalendar files and enterprise calendar auto-processing to place malicious links directly into employee schedules. Recent campaigns reportedly use ConsentFix, also described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication protections after the user interaction stage. Netskope Threat Labs framed OAuth authorization abuse as a structural scaling problem, particularly as organizations adopt AI agents that rely on persistent tokens, broad permissions, opaque runtimes, and autonomous workflows. The article described a confused deputy scenario in which an attacker plants malicious instructions in data consumed by an agent, causing the agent to use its own valid OAuth token to perform attacker-directed actions. SpecterOps published research on automating indirect prompt injection testing against an agentic system with access to Notion search, shell execution, and web-fetching tools. The workflow used poisoned Notion content to progress from simple echo payloads to full-chain payloads involving command execution and HTTP exfiltration, while noting that newer Claude Sonnet model behavior detected some earlier payload styles more reliably. StepSecurity reported that the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading across npm and PyPI ecosystems, with execution paths tied to project opening, package installation, or package import. Miasma uses a small binding.gyp file in a technique described as “Phantom Gyp,” while Hades relies on an obfuscated import hook in init.py, creating visibility gaps for tools focused primarily on install-time checks, process behavior, or declared dependencies. Aikido highlighted npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, as a major package-manager security change because dependency install scripts will no longer run by default unless packages are allowlisted. The release is also expected to block Git dependencies and remote URL dependencies unless explicitly permitted, and to treat implicit node-gyp rebuilds triggered by binding.gyp as install scripts. Socket announced Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer, positioning the appointment around enterprise concerns with open source, AI tooling, and malicious package risk. The company said it now protects more than 27,000 organizations and framed the role as both an internal security function and a customer-facing software supply chain security leadership position. PRODAFT reporting cited by The Hacker News tracks The Gentlemen ransomware operation as Phantom Mantis and says the group shifted from affiliate activity into an independent partnership program in July 2025. The operation has claimed 478 victims, reportedly accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026, and includes Go-based ransomware that Microsoft tracks under Storm-2697 with a self-propagation mode when launched with the --spread argument. Law enforcement dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency service Europol said was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million. Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized domains, vehicles, properties, cryptocurrency assets, Telegram accounts, and retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records tied to alleged money mule accounts. In the U.K., Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected ICT systems, while students taking GCSE and A-Level exams were allowed onsite. The incident type was not confirmed, but the reporting cited nearly 2,000 education and childcare-sector cyber incidents recorded by the U.K. Information Commissioner’s Office between 2019 and 2025. Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court after transfer from Thailand, where he was arrested in November, over allegations that he supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard group. Prosecutors allege cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko funded infrastructure used in attacks, and an FBI affidavit identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies while suggesting the actual victim count is higher. In U.S. defense policy, a Senate Armed Services Committee amendment to create a standalone Cyber Force failed by a 14-13 vote. The debate reflected bipartisan frustration over cyber personnel readiness for U.S. Cyber Command, while the Senate bill separately proposed a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon CIO and principal cyber advisor.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust...

entry.title: ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, Coupang Fine, LangGraph CVEs, and AI Agent Risks
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of phishing and vishing tactics that continue to rely on trusted brands and user interaction. The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident involving external access to a significant amount of data affecting current and former students. ShinyHunters claimed on its extortion site that it obtained more than 40GB of material, including payment-related data, while HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data identified roughly 455,000 unique email addresses and associated personal, academic, and fee information. In South Korea, Coupang received a record 624.6 billion won, approximately $409 million, fine from the Personal Information Protection Commission after a breach affecting about 37.55 million people. The regulator cited authentication signature key management failures, access control deficiencies, data destruction and notification violations, and obstruction of the investigation; authorities identified a former Coupang IT employee, a 43-year-old Chinese national, as the primary suspect. Multiple reports emphasized that attackers are increasingly operating through identity systems rather than relying solely on traditional exploit chains. Flashpoint reported more than 11.1 million devices infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity artifacts circulating in criminal markets. AI agent security emerged as a recurring concern across SaaS, developer workflows, and CI/CD environments. Varonis found that the OpenClaw email agent Pinchy could be tricked by ordinary-looking phishing emails into forwarding sensitive material, while Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content. AppOmni described agentic AI as a new class of SaaS identity capable of fetching data, creating users, and modifying configurations. Related identity exposure analysis from XM Cyber highlighted the risk of unmanaged human, machine, service, cloud, and AI accounts across hybrid environments, particularly where credentials are reused, cached, weak, or poorly governed. Check Point Research disclosed three vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing implementations, including CVE-2025-67644, a SQLite checkpointer SQL injection, and CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue. The research showed that SQL injection could be chained with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution where applications expose getstatehistory() with attacker-controlled filters; fixes are available in updated LangGraph and checkpoint packages. CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, requiring U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities under accelerated timelines, with the shortest deadline set at three days. Prioritization is based on factors including public exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog status, automation potential, and whether exploitation grants partial or full system control. The broader vulnerability management discussion reflected shrinking exploit windows. The Hacker News cited reporting that AI-assisted discovery has increased vulnerability volume and reduced average time-to-exploit, while Aikido’s Docker Hardened Images integration illustrated parallel efforts to reduce container vulnerability noise through signed SBOMs and Docker VEX attestations. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing email posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270. The message used an empty subject line, a JPEG attachment, and a callback number, with OCR revealing a brand mismatch between Geek Squad and “Windows Defender” copyright text. Industrial security and industry community activity also featured in the window. Radiflow and Barrier announced an OT security networking dinner in Aberdeen focused on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and critical infrastructure security, while The Hacker News published the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners across 95 subcategories.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of...

entry.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, and npm Supply Chain Controls
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability updates affecting BitLocker recovery behavior and continued attention to operational technology visibility gaps. Attackers are reportedly targeting CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry that enables code execution with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. Ivanti patched the vulnerability in Sentry versions R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1, but Shadowserver reported soon after that exposed vulnerable systems had already been backdoored in some cases. Shadowserver observed 19 vulnerable Ivanti Sentry instances during scanning and said at least two were backdoored, warning that unpatched exposed systems were likely compromised. The report adds to a broader Ivanti risk pattern: CISA has flagged 34 vulnerabilities across Ivanti products as actively exploited in the wild, including 12 that were also targeted in ransomware attacks. The University of Nottingham confirmed unauthorized access to its student records system, affecting current students and alumni. Have I Been Pwned reported that 454,600 individuals were impacted, with exposed data including email addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility and said it stole more than 40GB of documents from the University of Nottingham and its Malaysia and China campuses. The university said it is working with the third party that maintains the affected platform on a forensic investigation and has reported the incident to Action Fraud and the UK Information Commissioner’s Office. BleepingComputer linked the intrusion to a wider ShinyHunters campaign targeting cloud and on-premises Oracle PeopleSoft instances at more than 100 organizations globally. The incident highlights continued actor interest in enterprise student information systems and third-party-maintained platforms containing high-volume personal and administrative records. GitHub announced breaking changes planned for npm version 12 that will disable dependency install scripts by default. The change targets a long-standing supply chain risk in which npm install can execute preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts from transitive dependencies, allowing a compromised package anywhere in the dependency tree to run arbitrary code on developer systems or CI runners. Under the npm 12 model, dependency install scripts will not execute unless explicitly approved at the project level. npm install will also stop resolving Git dependencies or dependencies from remote URLs unless those behaviors are explicitly allowed with relevant options, narrowing default execution and resolution pathways commonly abused in dependency-chain compromise scenarios. Microsoft also resolved a Windows platform issue that caused some Windows Server 2025 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after the April 2026 security update. The bug affected devices with BitLocker enabled on the OS drive and a specific unrecommended Group Policy configuration involving TPM platform validation and PCR7; fixes were released in KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025 and KB5093998 for Windows 11 23H2. A webinar titled “Closing the OT Security Visibility Gap: From Blind Spots to Continuous Protection” focuses on persistent visibility limitations in operational technology environments. The framing reflects a broader security concern around insufficient asset, traffic, and behavioral visibility across industrial networks where continuous monitoring remains harder than in conventional IT environments. The topic aligns with ongoing pressure on defenders to understand OT exposure and detect changes in environments that often include legacy systems, limited instrumentation, and operational constraints. While the article is webinar-focused rather than incident-driven, it reflects continued industry emphasis on closing monitoring gaps in critical infrastructure and industrial settings.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability...

entry.title: MCP Security Tradeoffs for Agentic SOC Access
entry.content: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new implications for credential exposure and access control. Mitiga’s analysis distinguishes APIs and MCP by their intended consumers. APIs are described as contracts for code, best suited to deterministic automation where developers know which endpoints to call and require predictable behavior. MCP, by contrast, is presented as a contract for AI agents that need to discover, select, and chain tools during live investigative tasks. The article positions APIs as better aligned with scripted workflows, integrations, and repeatable automation. MCP is framed as more useful for analysts, AI assistants, and Agentic SOC platforms operating through natural language prompts, where the agent determines which tools to use and how to combine outputs during an investigation. A key reported benefit of MCP is reduced time-to-first-answer in security investigations. The article states that MCP can allow an LLM-driven system to choose tools, chain results, and format outputs without requiring a pre-built workflow, potentially compressing early investigative steps into minutes. The same access model also expands the security surface of AI-enabled SOC operations. Mitiga notes that MCP servers provide agents with credentialed access into security environments, making MCP tokens, scopes, and server permissions part of the effective attack surface. In this framing, MCP adoption introduces identity and authorization considerations similar to API security, but applied to autonomous or semi-autonomous agent activity. The dominant theme is the coexistence of deterministic automation and agentic investigation models. APIs remain central for predictable machine-to-machine execution, while MCP is presented as an emerging interface layer for AI-assisted security operations that can accelerate analysis while concentrating privileged access paths around agent tooling.
entry.summary: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new...

entry.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...

entry.title: JDY Botnet, Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, and Critical Ivanti/Fortinet/SAP Patches
entry.content: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive cyberattack affecting Australian sugar production. Multiple articles focused on how AI agents challenge existing zero trust and security inspection models. Varonis and Netskope both described AI agents as non-human actors that authenticate, call APIs, access databases, invoke tools, and exchange data at machine speed, creating gaps in policies designed around human access and authentication-time controls. Reported threat scenarios included prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, supply chain compromise, and uninspected Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM traffic. Software supply chain exposure in AI-assisted development also featured prominently. Socket announced an integration with Replit intended to evaluate open source packages as they enter AI-powered build workflows, citing threats such as typosquatting, impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that retrieve second-stage payloads. The reporting reflects a broader shift in which AI agents can recommend, install, and wire dependencies directly into applications. SpecterOps published research on SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, demonstrating offensive use cases involving spinvokeexternalrestendpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AIGENERATEEMBEDDINGS. The proof-of-concept activity included HTTPS exfiltration of table contents and files, trigger-based persistence for newly inserted data, NTLM coercion over SMB via UNC paths for ONNX models, and command-and-control patterns disguised as embedding-style traffic. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 stood out because it is reported as unpatched and under active exploitation. Tenable described the flaw as a path traversal issue in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint caused by insufficient sanitization of multipart filename values, while VulnCheck reported that default unauthenticated auto-login can allow attackers to obtain a session token and reach remote code execution. Censys data cited in the reporting identified roughly 7,000 internet-exposed Langflow instances, with most in North America. Patch reporting covered several critical enterprise products. Fortinet fixed CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection flaw in FortiSandbox products; Ivanti fixed CVE-2026-10520, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated root-level command injection issue in Ivanti Sentry, and CVE-2026-10523, a CVSS 9.9 authentication bypass enabling creation of arbitrary administrative accounts. SAP also released fixes for critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and NetWeaver Application Server Java, with no exploitation reported for those listed issues. CISA added Cisco, Chrome, and Arista vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The additions included CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, CVE-2026-11645 in Google Chrome V8, and CVE-2026-7473 in Arista EOS. Arista stated it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 due to potential configuration breakage and instead documented ACL-based mitigations. Black Lotus Labs reporting, covered by both The Hacker News and BleepingComputer, described the China-linked JDY botnet as having expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet is used as a centrally controlled reconnaissance network for service discovery, banner grabbing, TLS certificate collection, protocol fingerprinting, and flaw-focused scanning after public vulnerability disclosures. The botnet has expanded beyond earlier Cisco RV320 and RV325 router activity to include devices from Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, Linksys, and Cisco. Researchers said JDY places strong emphasis on U.S. targets, including U.S. military and associated networks. Command infrastructure reportedly uses Tor hidden services, and some activity involves the open-source Platypus reverse-shell and host-management framework. A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar operations in Queensland, forcing shutdowns at the Farleigh and Racecourse mills and prompting growers to stop harvesting sugarcane until further communication. The company said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities, implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running, and was working to restore affected systems. Mackay Sugar did not disclose whether ransomware, data theft, or another form of malicious activity was involved. Identity and access management remained a parallel theme, particularly around third parties and account verification. Health-ISAC released a white paper on third-party IAM risks in healthcare, emphasizing vendor, partner, remote access, connected device, and facility management complexity. Separate reporting on identity verification cited a 160% surge in credential theft in 2025 and described phishing-resistant MFA, service desk verification, device trust signals, and passkeys as major areas of focus in modern identity programs.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive...
